column2: The Bureau of Land Management's Cedar City District proposes a livestock grazing management program on about 2,567,466 acres of public land across roughly 210 allotments in Kane, Garfield, and Washington Counties, Utah, and Coconino County, Arizona, known as the Kanab/Escalante (K/E) area. The preferred Alternative 5 would apply specific grazing systems on 129 allotments, continuous seasonal management on 60, and eliminate grazing on 21, starting at 68,298 AUMs for livestock and growing to 91,444 AUMs over 24 years. Implementation was scheduled for the March 1981 grazing fee year.

column2: The plan area is largely rural, with ranching families in Kane and Garfield Counties who have grazed cattle since the 1870s and whose lifestyles and identities are tied to livestock. About 282 ranch operations (small, medium, and large scale) hold BLM permits, with BLM forage providing roughly 40 percent of their annual feed and most permittees living in towns such as Boulder, Escalante, Kanab, Glendale, Orderville, Panguitch, and Tropic. Agriculture provides under 15 percent of employment and less than 5 percent of personal income, and both counties have seen population declines, while recre…

Six alternatives are analyzed: (1) Continuation of Present Management at 109,708 AUMs of grazing preference; (2) Elimination of Livestock Grazing, allocating all forage to wildlife and wild horses; (3) Multiple Resource Enhancement, sharply reducing grazing to protect watersheds and riparian zones; (4) Adjustment to Grazing Capacity at 68,298 AUMs with limited new developments; (5) the BLM-preferred Rangeland Management Recommendation, growing from 68,298 to 91,444 AUMs through grazing systems, water and fence developments, and vegetation treatments; and (6) Livestock Optimization, reaching a…

Inventories show soils, vegetation, and wildlife are mostly in poor to fair condition, with about 38 percent of suitable rangeland in poor forage condition, 12 percent of soils in severe or critical erosion, 44 percent of riparian miles in poor condition, and big-game habitat largely degraded with mule deer numbers down to about 5,539 from 17,044. Continuing present management would worsen forage, soils, and deer habitat, while eliminating grazing would substantially improve riparian areas, soils (211,366 acres of critical erosion improved), water quality, and wildlife but at high cost to ran…

Sixteen scoping meetings (April–November 1979) and public hearings in Kanab and Escalante (June 1980) drew testimony from federal and state agencies, county commissions, ranchers, and conservation groups, with a public comment period extended from 45 to 60 days. Ranchers, the Kane and Garfield County Commissions, and Utah's Governor strongly opposed proposed AUM reductions, citing prior cumulative cuts (up to 68–71 percent for some permittees), unfulfilled BLM commitments to seedings and water developments, drought-skewed inventory data, understated economic impacts, and rejection of grazing …
